4: Hezekiah’s destruction of the Nehushtan — hûʾ kittēt ʾet naḥaš hannᵉḥōšet ʾăšer ʿāśāh mōšeh kî ʿaḏ hayyāmîm hāhēm hāyû bᵉnê yiśrāʾēl mᵉqaṭṭᵉrîm lô wayyiqrāʾ lô nᵉḥūštān, ‘he broke in pieces the bronze serpent that Moses had made, for until those days the people of Israel had been burning incense to it; it was called Nehushtan’ — documents the transformation of a legitimate Mosaic cult-object into an idolatrous cult-object. The bronze serpent of Numbers 21:8-9 was explicitly commanded by YHWH as a healing instrument; looking at it preserved life from the serpent-plague. The object itself was not idolatrous. Over time (ʿaḏ hayyāmîm hāhēm, ‘until those days’) it accumulated a cult: incense-burning, the hallmark of divine worship. The object’s history illustrates the OT’s relentless pattern: YHWH-commanded object → legitimate use → accumulated veneration → idolatry → destruction. The name nᵉḥūštān is either Hezekiah’s dismissive designation (a thing of bronze, a neḥōšet) or its actual cult name — the narrator’s irony is that an object named for its material rather than for YHWH is not worth worshipping. John 3:14-15 (‘as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life’) reclaims the original Mosaic serpent as a type of Christ’s crucifixion — restoring the typological meaning after its centuries of idolatrous overlay: the object that should have pointed forward to Christ was instead worshipped; Christ is the fulfillment of what the object originally signified.
13: The Sennacherib siege of Judah — ûḇišnat ʾarbaʿ ʿeśrēh šānāh lammelek ḥizqiyyāhû ʿālāh sannᵉḥērîḇ melek ʾaššûr ʿal kol ʿārê yᵉhûḏāh haḇṣûrôt wayyitpᵉśēm, ‘in the fourteenth year of King Hezekiah, Sennacherib king of Assyria came up against all the fortified cities of Judah and took them’ — is one of the most extensively documented events in the entire OT by external sources. The Taylor Prism (British Museum, discovered 1830) is Sennacherib’s own annalistic account of his 701 BCE campaign: he claims to have captured 46 of Hezekiah’s fortified cities, deported 200,150 people, and shut Hezekiah up in Jerusalem ‘like a bird in a cage.’ The Prism confirms the tribute paid (v14-16) and the siege of Jerusalem — while notably not claiming to have taken the city, consistent with 2 Kings 19:35-36’s account of the army’s destruction. The Lachish reliefs (Sennacherib’s palace at Nineveh, now in the British Museum) depict the siege and capture of Lachish (2 Kings 18:17: the Rabshakeh sent from Lachish), providing visual documentation of the Assyrian military operations described in 2 Kgs 18-19. The convergence of the Hebrew narrative, the Taylor Prism, and the Lachish reliefs makes the Hezekiah siege one of the best-attested episodes in biblical archaeology.
1:
v1 — Chronological synchronism: Hezekiah's accession
The accession of Hezekiah is synchronized with the third year of Hoshea king of Israel. The dual-synchronism system (northern king dated by the southern and vice versa) reflects the practice of the Deuteronomistic Historian working from parallel court records. Hezekiah's dates are debated: the dominant scholarly reconstruction places his accession c. 715 BCE, though some put it as early as 727 BCE based on internal biblical synchronisms. The chronological discrepancies in the Kings data are among the most discussed in OT scholarship.
2:
v2 — Twenty-five years old; twenty-nine year reign; mother Abi
The regnal formula for Hezekiah: age at accession (25), length of reign (29 years), mother's name (Abi/Abijah daughter of Zechariah). The Deuteronomistic regnal formula has been applied consistently throughout Kings — age, reign length, mother's name (for Judean kings), and theological evaluation. 'Abi daughter of Zechariah' — the Zechariah is likely distinct from the prophet; the Judean court practice of recording the queen mother's name (gevirah) reflects her institutional significance in the Judean monarchy.
3:
v3 — Theological evaluation: did right as David did
Hezekiah receives the highest possible Deuteronomistic commendation: he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD ke-khol asher asah David aviv — just as David his ancestor did. David is the standard against which all Judean kings are measured; only Hezekiah (here) and Josiah (2 Kings 22:2) receive unqualified Davidic comparison. This evaluation is unique even among the “good” kings, most of whom receive the qualified formula “right in the eyes of the LORD, though the high places were not removed.”
5:
v5 — Unique superlative: no king like him before or after
The superlative claim for Hezekiah — “among all the kings of Judah there was none like him, neither before nor after him” — is precisely the same formula used for Solomon (1 Kings 3:12) and later for Josiah (2 Kings 23:25). The repetition of this absolute formula for three kings is a literary tension within Kings itself; commentators have long noted that the same formula cannot logically apply to all three. The Deuteronomist's purpose seems to be situational superlative: Hezekiah surpasses all others in trust; Josiah in covenant-adherence; Solomon in wisdom.
6:
v6 — He clung to the LORD; did not depart from his commandments
The phrase dabaq ba-YHWH (he clung to / adhered to the LORD) is Deuteronomic covenant language (Deuteronomy 10:20; 2 Kings 11:22; 2 Kings 13:4; 2 Kings 30:20): the word dabaq (cling/cleave) describes the proper covenantal posture. The same word is used in Genesis 2:24 (husband clings to wife) — covenant loyalty has the intensity of marital commitment. Hezekiah is described as the model of covenant davaq. The negative counterpart — not turning aside from the commandments given to Moses — echoes the Deuteronomic formula of Joshua 23:6.
7:
v7 — The LORD was with him; he rebelled against Assyria
The divine-presence formula ve-YHWH imo (the LORD was with him) appears for Hezekiah just as it appeared for David and Joseph. In the ANE framework, royal success was interpreted as divine favor; for the Deuteronomist, this formula means covenant blessing is operationally present. The rebellion against Assyria — marat be-melekh Ashur (he rebelled against the king of Assyria) — refers to Hezekiah's decision to withhold tribute as part of his anti-Assyrian coalition (c. 705 BCE, when Sargon II died and Sennacherib's accession created instability throughout the empire).
8:
v8 — Hezekiah defeats Philistines to Gaza
Hezekiah's Philistine campaign (from watchtower to fortified city — a merism for the full range of settlements) demonstrates military strength consistent with his independence from Assyrian control. Archaeological excavations at sites like Tell es-Safi (Gath), Ashdod, and Ekron show destruction layers potentially linked to this campaign. The Assyrian records describe Padi king of Ekron as a pro-Assyrian vassal whom Hezekiah imprisoned — part of the anti-Assyrian coalition that preceded Sennacherib's 701 campaign.
9:
v9 — The fall of Samaria reviewed (Shalmaneser's siege, fourth year of Hezekiah)
The narrator inserts a retrospective review of Samaria's fall (cf. 2 Kings 17:3-6), synchronizing it with Hezekiah's reign. In the fourth year of Hezekiah (= seventh year of Hoshea), Shalmaneser V besieged Samaria. The dual synchronism frames the Northern Kingdom's end as a backdrop to Hezekiah's reform — what happened to the north is the threat the south faces if it does not reform. Shalmaneser V (727–722 BCE) began the siege; Sargon II (722–705 BCE) claims the conquest in his inscriptions.
10:
v10 — After three years, Samaria fell in the sixth year of Hezekiah
The three-year siege (724–722 BCE) ended in Samaria's fall — the sixth year of Hezekiah in the Deuteronomist's reckoning. This synchronism is significant: Sargon II's Annals record the deportation of 27,290 inhabitants of Samaria. The capital of the Northern Kingdom ceased to exist as an Israelite city; it became an Assyrian provincial capital (Samerina). The Deuteronomist's theological interpretation follows immediately in v12: the fall was the result of covenant violation.
11:
v11 — The deportation: Halah, Habor, Gozan, Median cities
The deportation destinations — Halah (Chalach), the Habor River (Nahar Gozan), and Median cities — are identifiable in Assyrian records. Halah is possibly Chalcis in northern Mesopotamia; the Habor (mod. Khabur River in Syria) was a well-known deportation zone; Gozan (mod. Tell Halaf) is confirmed as an Assyrian administrative center by excavations. The “Median cities” reflect Assyrian policy of populating the eastern frontier with deportees. This is the Assyrian māt Māda (land of the Medes), where deported Israelites were settled as buffer-population.
12:
v12 — Theological explanation: they violated the covenant of Moses
The Deuteronomistic explanation: Samaria fell because the people did not listen to the LORD and violated his covenant — et kol asher tzivah Moshe eved YHWH (all that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded). This is the Deuteronomistic History's master-key: national catastrophe is the inevitable consequence of covenant violation. The theologian-historian is not claiming that Samaria’s fall was surprising — it was the predicted outcome of the covenant curses in Deuteronomy 28. History and theology are integrated: geopolitical events are covenantal events.
14:
v14 — Hezekiah submits to Sennacherib at Lachish
After Sennacherib's invasion captures all the fortified cities of Judah, Hezekiah capitulates and sends tribute. Lachish (Lakhish) was Sennacherib's military headquarters for the Judean campaign — not Jerusalem, which he apparently chose not to assault directly after the tribute payment and subsequent events. The Lachish siege is one of the best-documented events of the OT era: Sennacherib's palace at Nineveh (modern Kuyunjik) contained a large relief showing the siege in detail, now in the British Museum (Room 36). Hezekiah's words — “I have sinned; withdraw and I will pay whatever you impose” — are a formal capitulation formula.
15:
v15 — All the silver from the temple and treasury handed over
Hezekiah empties the temple treasury and royal treasury of silver. Sennacherib's Annals (Taylor Prism) record the tribute: “30 talents of gold, 800 talents of silver, precious stones, antimony, large cuts of red stone, couches inlaid with ivory, elephant hides, ebony-wood, boxwood and all kinds of valuable treasures.” The MT of Kings says 300 talents of silver — Sennacherib says 800. The discrepancy (a ×2.67 difference) is discussed by scholars: different talent standards, different counting methods, or the Assyrian record including tribute collected over multiple periods are all proposed.
16:
v16 — Gold stripped from temple doors and doorposts
Hezekiah goes further — stripping the gold overlay (tzipui) from the temple doors and doorposts that he himself had plated. The verb qitztzetz (to cut off/strip) indicates a physical removal process. The doors and doorposts (daliyot ha-hekhal ve-et ha-imot) likely refer to the monumental cedar-and-gold entrance of Solomon's temple described in 1 Kings 6-7. The gold that took generations to accumulate is stripped in a crisis. Sennacherib's Annals record receiving this precious metal as part of the tribute, confirming the general picture.
17:
v17 — The three Assyrian officials and the delegation to Jerusalem
Sennacherib sends three high officials — the Tartan, Rab-saris, and Rabshakeh — from Lachish to Jerusalem. These are Assyrian titles, not personal names: turtānu (field marshal/second-in-command), rāb ša rēši (palace chief, possibly the chief eunuch), and rāb šāqê (chief cupbearer/officer, often serving as a diplomatic envoy). The delegation comes with a chayil kaved (heavy/massive force) — a military escort sufficient to intimidate. They stop at the Fuller's Field (sedeh ha-kavvas) — the same site where Isaiah confronted Ahaz (Isaiah 7:3).
18:
v18 — The Judean delegation: Eliakim, Shebna, Joah
The Judean negotiating team consists of three court officials: Eliakim son of Hilkiah (palace administrator, asher al ha-bayit), Shebna the secretary (sofer), and Joah son of Asaph the recorder (mazkir). Isaiah 22:15-25 contains an oracle specifically about Shebna and Eliakim — Shebna was rebuked as overreaching his station, and Eliakim was promised his place. That oracle appears to precede 2 Kings 18 chronologically; here we see Eliakim in his new role after Shebna's demotion from palace administrator to secretary. These are documented court officials whose titles match the administrative vocabulary of the period.
19:
v19 — Rabshakeh's opening challenge: 'What is the basis of this trust?'
The Rabshakeh delivers his message in the third person on behalf of the “great king, the king of Assyria” (ha-melekh ha-gadol melekh Ashur) — the standard Assyrian royal title used in official documents. The question 'al-mah habitachon ha-zeh' (what is this confidence?) is a rhetorical assault on Judah's basis for resistance. The speech is psychologically sophisticated propaganda: it analyzes and systematically destroys each possible source of trust (Egypt, military capacity, the LORD) before offering an alternative (Assyrian benevolence). The Rabshakeh speaks Hebrew fluently — evidence of Assyria's use of diplomats who could communicate directly with subject populations.
20:
v20 — Empty words: strategy and military strength without substance
The Rabshakeh dismisses Hezekiah's talk of strategy and military power as devar sefatayim (words of lips only — empty talk). This rhetorical move — identifying apparent strength as illusory — is classic siege warfare psychology. The Assyrian empire had developed sophisticated propaganda techniques to undermine the morale of besieged cities before assault. The Rabshakeh's speech can be read as a textbook example of ancient psychological warfare, designed to create defection and capitulation without costly direct assault.
21:
v21 — Egypt is a broken reed: the Nile staff metaphor
The comparison of Egypt to a qaneh haratzutz (broken reed / cracked staff) is a vivid image of unreliable support. A reed staff that shatters when leaned upon not only fails to support — it wounds the hand that trusts it. This image is also used in Ezekiel 29:6-7 (Egypt as a “staff of reed”). The Rabshakeh's political analysis proved accurate: Egypt's 25th dynasty support was inconsistent, and Tirhakah's forces (v9) were turned back by Sennacherib without decisive engagement. Politically, Egypt regularly encouraged small Levantine states to revolt against Assyria but consistently failed to provide adequate military backing.
22:
v22 — Twisted logic: the LORD whose high places Hezekiah removed
The Rabshakeh's most sophisticated argument: Hezekiah removed the high places and altars, so where will people worship? — implying the LORD is angered by Hezekiah's reforms. This argument reflects either misunderstanding of Yahwistic theology (not distinguishing the high-place removal as centralization to Jerusalem) or deliberate distortion. The Rabshakeh's awareness of Hezekiah's reforms is historically plausible — Assyrian intelligence networks monitored subject states. The argument was effective propaganda precisely because it exploited popular attachment to local worship sites that Hezekiah had abolished.
23:
v23 — The horse wager: can you find two thousand riders?
The Rabshakeh's mock offer — I will give you 2,000 horses if you can find riders — targets a specific Judean military weakness. Judah's cavalry was limited; Israel's horse procurement (which Solomon began) had been ongoing, but Judah remained less cavalry-heavy than the major powers. The taunt that Judah cannot field enough trained cavalry to mount even 2,000 horses exposes the military disproportion between a small Levantine kingdom and the Assyrian superpower, whose cavalry forces numbered in the tens of thousands.
24:
v24 — Cannot repel one of the least officers; Egypt's chariots are useless
The military argument continues: Judah cannot hold back even the least of Assyria's commanders, yet relies on Egypt for chariots and cavalry. The Egyptian alliance (which Hezekiah had pursued) is explicitly named as the military backup plan — and dismissed as inadequate. The Assyrian army in 701 BCE was the most powerful military force in the ancient Near East: Sennacherib's Annals describe the siege of 46 Judean cities. The Rabshakeh's military assessment of Judah's weakness was objectively accurate.
25:
v25 — The LORD commanded me to come against this land
The most audacious claim in the Rabshakeh's speech: YHWH amar elai aleh el ha-aretz ha-zot ve-hashchiteha — 'The LORD told me: go up against this land and destroy it.' Whether this reflects (1) knowledge of Isaiah's oracles about Assyria as the rod of God's anger (Isaiah 10:5-6), (2) genuine ANE political theology (all conquests are divinely authorized), or (3) pure bluff is debated. The claim creates maximum psychological confusion: if YHWH has commissioned this invasion, resistance is rebellion against the divine will. It appropriates the covenantal framework itself as a weapon against Hezekiah's piety.
26:
v26 — Speak in Aramaic, not Hebrew
The Judean officials' request to use Aramaic (Arami) rather than Hebrew (Yehudit — Judean/Hebrew) is revealing: they understood both languages, and so did the Rabshakeh. Aramaic was the diplomatic and commercial lingua franca of the late Assyrian empire — what Latin was to medieval Europe or English to modern diplomacy. Hebrew was the vernacular spoken by the people on the walls. The officials were trying to contain the Rabshakeh's psychological warfare to an elite diplomatic channel and prevent it from reaching the civilian population.
27:
v27 — The speech is for the men on the wall too
The Rabshakeh refuses to restrict his message to the officials — his audience is precisely the civilian population (ha-anashim ha-yoshvim al ha-chomah — the men sitting on the wall). The gruesome image that follows (eating their own excrement and drinking their own urine, v27) drives home the horror of a prolonged siege. This was sophisticated psychological warfare: the Rabshakeh was intentionally creating a wedge between the military/political leadership and the civilian population, hoping to generate popular pressure for surrender. The tactic of appealing directly to civilians over their leaders' heads is documented in other Assyrian diplomatic interactions.
28:
v28 — The Rabshakeh stands and shouts in Hebrew
The shift to public proclamation in Hebrew (Yehudit) is deliberate and devastating. Having been told to use Aramaic, the Rabshakeh does the opposite. The shout (va-yiqra be-qol gadol — crying with a great voice) to the assembled population on the walls is an act of psychological warfare targeting the will to resist. The content (vv29-35) is carefully structured: first, a warning against trusting Hezekiah; then an offer of peace and prosperity; finally, a historical argument that no god has ever defeated Assyria.
29:
v29 — 'Don't let Hezekiah mislead you; he cannot save you'
The Rabshakeh's direct attack on Hezekiah's authority: al yashia' lachem Chizkiyahu — 'Hezekiah cannot save you.' The word nashia' (deliver/save) is the vocabulary of divine deliverance — the Rabshakeh is using theological language to undermine Hezekiah's role as the trustworthy king. By framing Hezekiah as a deceiver (yashia' Hezekiah = he will lead you astray), the speech creates maximum suspicion of royal leadership at the moment when civilian trust in that leadership is most critical.
30:
v30 — 'Don't trust the LORD saying Jerusalem will not fall'
The speech directly targets Hezekiah's theological argument: his claim that the LORD will certainly rescue Jerusalem. The Rabshakeh quotes the Yahwistic promise accurately — “Jerusalem will certainly not fall to the king of Assyria” — and then implicitly ridicules it by juxtaposing it with the historical record of Assyrian conquest (vv33-35). The tactic is to make the theological promise of divine protection appear as dangerous self-deception, and Hezekiah's reliance on it as royal irresponsibility toward the civilian population.
31:
v31 — 'Make peace with me; eat from your vine and fig tree'
The offer of terms: ashu itti (make a blessing/peace with me — the word is berakhah, blessing). The promise to each person eating from his vine and fig tree and cistern is a deliberate echo of Solomon's golden age (1 Kings 4:25) — the Rabshakeh is using Israel's own covenantal blessing-language to describe Assyrian benevolence. The propaganda is sophisticated: it inverts the covenant promises, suggesting that submission to Assyria will provide exactly what faithfulness to YHWH promised. This is imperial theology appropriating Israelite imagery.
32:
v32 — 'I will take you to a land like your own — choose life'
The deportation is presented as a positive option: a land of grain, wine, bread, vineyards, olive trees, and honey — bacharu va-chayyu ve-al temotu (choose and live and do not die). The phrase “a land like your own” is a deliberate echo of Deuteronomy’s promised land descriptions. The “choose life” (bachar va-chayyu) echoes Moses' own language in Deuteronomy 30:19: “Choose life.” The Rabshakeh's speech appropriates Mosaic covenant language to argue for submission to Assyria — one of the most audacious rhetorical moves in the biblical text.
33:
v33 — 'Has any god ever rescued his land from Assyria?'
The comparative theology argument: has any god of the nations delivered his land from Assyrian power? This argument reflects genuine Assyrian imperial theology — Assur, the chief Assyrian deity, was understood as superior to all other national gods. Assyrian royal inscriptions regularly describe conquest as Assur's triumph over the gods of conquered nations. The Rabshakeh's argument was historically compelling: Assyria had indeed conquered nation after nation whose gods did not protect them. The theological challenge is real: if YHWH is just another national deity, the historical record is against Judah.
34:
v34 — Gods of Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim named and mocked
The roster of defeated gods and nations: Hamath (on the Orontes, conquered by Sargon II in 720 BCE), Arpad (near Aleppo, conquered by Tiglath-Pileser III in 740 BCE), Sepharvaim (possibly Sibraim in Syria), Hena and Ivvah (locations uncertain). Each named city represents a national god who failed to prevent Assyrian conquest. The implicit argument: if the gods of Hamath and Arpad could not prevent their nations’ destruction, why should YHWH be different? The challenge to YHWH's uniqueness is what makes chapter 19’s response so important theologically.
35:
v35 — 'How will the LORD rescue Jerusalem from me?'
The climax of the Rabshakeh's speech: the direct challenge to YHWH. The question is unanswerable on human political grounds — no military force in the Levant could match Assyria. The speech works by systematically eliminating every possible human source of trust and then turning that logic against YHWH as if he were merely another national deity. The Hezekiah narrative's response (through Isaiah's oracle in 2 Kings 19) will argue precisely that YHWH is categorically different from the gods of Hamath and Arpad — not a national deity among others but the creator of heaven and earth.
36:
v36 — The people remained silent; the king had commanded silence
Ve-haamu hacharishu — 'the people were silent.' The silence is disciplined, not cowardly — Hezekiah had ordered them not to answer (al ta'anu oto davar). The tactic of ordered silence in the face of propaganda was wise: any response would be used by the Rabshakeh as further material for argument. The wall-sitting population's enforced silence prevented the psychological wedge from being driven between citizen and king. The contrast with the Rabshakeh's loud shouting creates a dramatic tableau: Assyrian noise versus Judean silence, the external voice of worldly power versus the inner quiet of those who wait on God.
37:
v37 — The delegation returns to Hezekiah with torn garments
Eliakim, Shebna, and Joah return to Hezekiah with qeru'ei vegadim (torn garments) and report the Rabshakeh's words. The torn garments are the sign of grief and distress — the officials have internalized the gravity of what they heard. The report to Hezekiah sets the scene for 2 Kings 19: a king who receives terrible news and takes it immediately to the house of the LORD (2 Kings 19:1). The same three officials who went to negotiate will now serve as messengers to Isaiah, connecting the diplomatic and prophetic responses.
